is a [piston engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine#Reciprocating_engines "Internal combustion engine") where the cylinders are located on either side of a central [crankshaft](/wiki/Crankshaft "Crankshaft"). Flat engines are also known as **horizontally opposed engines**, however this is distinct from the less common [opposed-piston engine](/wiki/Opposed-piston_engine "Opposed-piston engine") design, whereby each cylinder has two pistons sharing a central combustion chamber.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Combustion engine using pistons facing to the sides on a common crankshaft

For similarly-named engines with two pistons per cylinder, see [opposed-piston engine](/wiki/Opposed-piston_engine "Opposed-piston engine").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Boxerengineanimation.gif/500px-Boxerengineanimation.gif)](/wiki/File:Boxerengineanimation.gif)

Animation (with errors) of a boxer engine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Benz_Contramotor_IMG_1343_2023-11-25_JM.jpg/250px-Benz_Contramotor_IMG_1343_2023-11-25_JM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benz_Contramotor_IMG_1343_2023-11-25_JM.jpg)

Benz Contramotor, the first commercial flat-engine design, ca. 1899

A **flat engine** is a [piston engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine#Reciprocating_engines "Internal combustion engine") where the cylinders are located on either side of a central [crankshaft](/wiki/Crankshaft "Crankshaft"). Flat engines are also known as **horizontally opposed engines**, however this is distinct from the less common [opposed-piston engine](/wiki/Opposed-piston_engine "Opposed-piston engine") design, whereby each cylinder has two pistons sharing a central combustion chamber.

The most common configuration of flat engines is the [boxer engine](#Boxer_configuration) configuration, in which the pistons of each opposed pair of cylinders move inwards and outwards at the same time. The other configuration is effectively a [V engine](/wiki/V_engine "V engine") with a 180-degree angle between the cylinder banks: in this configuration each pair of cylinders shares a single crankpin, so that as one piston moves inward, the other moves outward.

The first flat engine (Benz Contramotor) was built in 1897 by [Karl Benz](/wiki/Karl_Benz "Karl Benz"). Flat engines have been used in aviation, motorcycle and automobile applications. They are now less common in cars than [straight engines](/wiki/Straight_engine "Straight engine") (for engines with fewer than six cylinders) and [V engines](/wiki/V_engine "V engine") (for engines with six or more cylinders). Flat engines are more common in aircraft, where straight engines are a rarity and V engines have almost vanished except in historical aircraft. They have even replaced [radial engines](/wiki/Radial_engine "Radial engine") in many smaller installations.

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Flat_engine "Special:EditPage/Flat engine") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(February 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Moteurs_%C3%A0_Plat_-_V_%C3%A0_180%C2%B0_vs._Boxer.png/250px-Moteurs_%C3%A0_Plat_-_V_%C3%A0_180%C2%B0_vs._Boxer.png)](/wiki/File:Moteurs_%C3%A0_Plat_-_V_%C3%A0_180%C2%B0_vs._Boxer.png)

Difference between two flat 6 cylinder engines: 180° V on the left, boxer on the right

The advantages of flat engines are a short length, low [centre of mass](/wiki/Centre_of_mass "Centre of mass") and suitability for [air cooling](/wiki/Air-cooled_engine "Air-cooled engine") (due to the well-exposed, large-surface-area cylinders and cylinder heads, and their short length).

Compared with the more common [straight engines](/wiki/Straight_engine "Straight engine"), flat engines have better [primary balance](/wiki/Primary_balance "Primary balance") (resulting in less vibration);[[1]](#cite_note-1) however, the disadvantages are increased width and the need to have two cylinder heads. Compared with [V engines](/wiki/V_engine "V engine") – the most common layout for engines with six cylinders or more – flat engines again have a lower centre of mass, and, for six-cylinders, better primary balance; the disadvantage is again their being wider.[[2]](#cite_note-why-subaru-2)

The most common usages of flat engines are:

* [Flat-twin engines](/wiki/Flat-twin_engine "Flat-twin engine") are mostly used in motorcycles. Occasionally they have been used in light cars, aircraft and industrial applications, mostly up until the 1960s.
* [Flat-four engines](/wiki/Flat-four_engine "Flat-four engine") are mostly used in cars (most notably in the earlier [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") [Types 1](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle") to [4](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_4 "Volkswagen Type 4"), and by [Subaru](/wiki/Subaru "Subaru")[[2]](#cite_note-why-subaru-2) in most of their models), and have occasionally been used in motorcycles. Their most common use is in smaller single-engine [general-aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") aircraft, for which they are still manufactured and used to this day.
* [Flat-six engines](/wiki/Flat-six_engine "Flat-six engine") are mostly used in aircraft and cars (particularly by the [Porsche](/wiki/Porsche "Porsche") 911 sports car), and have occasionally been used in motorcycles.
* [Flat-eight engines](/wiki/Flat-eight_engine "Flat-eight engine") have been used in several racing cars, mostly by Porsche in the 1960s.
* [Flat-ten engines](/wiki/Flat-ten_engine "Flat-ten engine") are not known to have reached production. A prototype road car engine was built by [Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet") in the 1960s.
* [Flat-twelve engines](/wiki/Flat-twelve_engine "Flat-twelve engine") have been used in various racing cars, notably the [Porsche 917K](/wiki/Porsche_917K "Porsche 917K"), during the 1960s and 1970s, and in [Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari") road cars from 1973 to 1996. The [Panhard EBR](/wiki/Panhard_EBR "Panhard EBR") armoured car is one of the few military vehicles to have used such an engine.
* [Flat-sixteen engines](/wiki/Flat-sixteen_engine "Flat-sixteen engine") are not known to have reached production. Prototype racing car engines were built by [Coventry Climax](/wiki/Coventry_Climax "Coventry Climax") and Porsche in the 1960s and 1970s.

### Boxer configuration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boxer configuration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/R68-opposed-cylinders.jpg/250px-R68-opposed-cylinders.jpg)](/wiki/File:R68-opposed-cylinders.jpg)

1954 [BMW R68](/wiki/BMW_R68 "BMW R68") flat-twin boxer engine

Most flat engines use a "boxer" configuration, where each pair of opposing pistons move inwards and outwards at the same time, somewhat like boxing competitors punching their gloves together before a fight.[[3]](#cite_note-Bonk-3) Boxer engines have low vibration, being the only common configuration that has no unbalanced forces, regardless of the number of pairs of cylinders. Boxer engines therefore do not require either a [balance shaft](/wiki/Balance_shaft "Balance shaft")[[4]](#cite_note-Hanlon-4) or counterweights on the [crankshaft](/wiki/Crankshaft "Crankshaft") to balance the weight of the reciprocating parts. However, a [rocking couple](/wiki/Couple_(mechanics)#Applications "Couple (mechanics)") is present, since each cylinder is slightly offset from the other member of its pair due to the distance between the [crankpins](/wiki/Crankpin "Crankpin") along the crankshaft.[[3]](#cite_note-Bonk-3)

### 180-degree V engine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 180-degree V engine")]

An alternative configuration for flat engines is as a 180-degree [V engine](/wiki/V_engine "V engine"), which has been used on most twelve-cylinder flat engines. In this configuration, each pair of pistons shares a [crankpin](/wiki/Crankpin "Crankpin"), this being simpler than the boxer configuration, where each piston has its own separate crankpin.

## Aviation use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aviation use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Puch_Flugmotor_Patent_AT_48877_1909-11-08.png/250px-Puch_Flugmotor_Patent_AT_48877_1909-11-08.png)](/wiki/File:Puch_Flugmotor_Patent_AT_48877_1909-11-08.png)

Twin-boxer of [Johann Puch](/wiki/Johann_Puch "Johann Puch"), Patent AT 48877 (1909)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Riedelanlasser.jpg/250px-Riedelanlasser.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riedelanlasser.jpg)

World War II-era Riedel starter motor

In 1902, the Pearse monoplane (which would later become one of the first aircraft to achieve flight) was powered by a flat-twin engine. Amongst the first commercially produced aircraft to use a flat engine was the 1909 [Santos-Dumont Demoiselle](/wiki/Santos-Dumont_Demoiselle "Santos-Dumont Demoiselle") range of aeroplanes, which was powered by boxer-twin engines.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Several boxer-four engines have been produced specifically for light aircraft. A number of manufacturers produced boxer-six aircraft engines during the 1930s and 1940s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During World War II, a boxer-twin engine called the "Riedel starter" was used as a starter motor/mechanical [APU](/wiki/Auxiliary_power_unit#As_mechanical_"startup"_APUs_for_jet_engines "Auxiliary power unit") for the early German jet engines, such as the [Junkers Jumo 004](/wiki/Junkers_Jumo_004 "Junkers Jumo 004") and [BMW 003](/wiki/BMW_003 "BMW 003"). Designed by [Norbert Riedel](/wiki/Norbert_Riedel "Norbert Riedel"), these engines have a very [oversquare](/wiki/Stroke_ratio#Oversquare_or_short-stroke_engine "Stroke ratio") stroke ratio of 2:1 so that they could fit within the intake diverter, directly forward of the turbine compressor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Motorcycle use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Motorcycle use")]

Flat engines offer several advantages for motorcycles including a low [centre of mass](/wiki/Centre_of_mass "Centre of mass"), low vibration, suitability for shaft drive, and equal cooling of the cylinders (for air-cooled engines).[[5]](#cite_note-English-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Szymezak1-6) The most common design of flat engine for motorcycles is the boxer-twin, beginning with the 1905 Fée flat-twin engine, manufactured by the Light Motors Company, which was the first production motorcycle engine.[[7]](#cite_note-TMCfuture-7) [BMW Motorrad](/wiki/BMW_Motorrad "BMW Motorrad") have a long history of boxer-twin motorcycles, beginning in 1923 with the [BMW R32](/wiki/BMW_R32 "BMW R32")[[5]](#cite_note-English-5)

Several motorcycles have been produced with flat-four engines, such as the 1938–1939 [Zündapp K800](/wiki/Z%C3%BCndapp "Zündapp") and the 1974–1987 [Honda Gold Wing](/wiki/Honda_Gold_Wing "Honda Gold Wing"). In 1987, the Honda Gold Wing engine was upsized to a flat-six design.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Automotive use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Automotive use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Diesel_Engine_Type_DS140_2C_Hino.jpg/250px-Diesel_Engine_Type_DS140_2C_Hino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diesel_Engine_Type_DS140_2C_Hino.jpg)

1969 [Hino Motors](/wiki/Hino_Motors "Hino Motors") DS140 boxer-twelve diesel engine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/ChassisT11.JPG/250px-ChassisT11.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChassisT11.JPG)

1923-1927 [Tatra 11](/wiki/Tatra_11 "Tatra 11") chassis and engine— front-mounted flat-twin engine with rear-wheel drive

When used in cars, advantages of flat engines are a low [centre of mass](/wiki/Centre_of_mass "Centre of mass") (which improves the handling of the car),[[4]](#cite_note-Hanlon-4) short length, low vibration and suitability for air cooling (due to the well exposed, large surface area, cylinder heads and short length).[[9]](#cite_note-9) However the larger width of flat engines (compared with the more common inline and V layouts) is a drawback, particularly when the engine is located between the steered wheels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Flat engines were used by various automobile manufacturers – mostly with a boxer-four design – up until the late 1990s. Since then, only Porsche and Subaru have remained as significant manufacturers of flat engines.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Drivetrain layout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flat_engine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Drivetrain layout")]

Due to the short length of flat engines, locating a flat engine outside of the car's [wheelbase](/wiki/Wheelbase "Wheelbase") results in minimal overhang.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENunney198812-10) Therefore, many cars with flat engines have used a [rear-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout](/wiki/Rear-engine,_rear-wheel-drive_layout "Rear-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout"). Examples include the flat-twin [BMW 600](/wiki/BMW_600 "BMW 600") (1957–1959) and [BMW 700](/wiki/BMW_700 "BMW 700") (1959–1965); the flat-four [Tatra 97](/wiki/Tatra_97 "Tatra 97") (1936–1939), [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle") (1938–2003) and [Porsche 356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356") (1948–1965); and the flat-six [Chevrolet Corvair](/wiki/Chevrolet_Corvair "Chevrolet Corvair") (1959–1969), [Porsche 911](/wiki/Porsche_911 "Porsche 911") (1963–present), and [Tucker 48](/wiki/Tucker_48 "Tucker 48") (1947–1948).

The opposite layout, [front-engine front-wheel drive](/wiki/Front-engine,_front-wheel-drive_layout "Front-engine, front-wheel-drive layout"), was also common for cars with flat engines. Examples include the [Citroën 2CV](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_2CV "Citroën 2CV") (1948–1990), [Panhard Dyna X](/wiki/Panhard_Dyna_X "Panhard Dyna X") (1948–1954), [Lancia Flavia](/wiki/Lancia_Flavia "Lancia Flavia") (1961–1970), [Citroën GS](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn_GS "Citroën GS") (1970–1986), [Alfa Romeo Alfasud](/wiki/Alfa_Romeo_Alfasud "Alfa Romeo Alfasud") (1971–1989) and [Subaru Leone](/wiki/Subaru_Leone "Subaru Leone") (1971–1994).

Subaru have been producing cars with a [front-engine, four-wheel-drive layout](/wiki/Front-engine,_four-wheel-drive_layout "Front-engine, four-wheel-drive layout") powered by flat engines (mostly boxer-four engines) since 1971. Examples include the [Subaru Leone](/wiki/Subaru_Leone "Subaru Leone") (1971–1994), [Subaru Legacy](/wiki/Subaru_Legacy "Subaru Legacy") (1989-2025) and [Subaru Impreza](/wiki/Subaru_Impreza "Subaru Impreza") (1992–present). The front half-shafts come out of a front differential that is part of the gearbox. A rear driveshaft connects the gearbox to the rear half-shafts.

The traditional [front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout](/wiki/Front-engine,_rear-wheel-drive_layout "Front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout") is relatively uncommon for cars with flat engines, however some examples include the [Toyota 86 / Subaru BRZ](/wiki/Toyota_86 "Toyota 86") (2012–present), [Jowett Javelin](/wiki/Jowett_Javelin "Jowett Javelin") (1947–1953), [Glas Isar](/wiki/Glas_Isar "Glas Isar") (1958–1965) and the [Tatra 11](/wiki/Tatra_11 "Tatra 11") (1923–1927).

Boxer-four engines

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Volkswagen_motor_cut_1945.JPG/250px-Volkswagen_motor_cut_1945.JPG)](/wiki/File:Volkswagen_motor_cut_1945.JPG)

1936–2006 [Volkswagen air-cooled](/wiki/Volkswagen_air-cooled_engine "Volkswagen air-cooled engine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Subaru_BRZ_engine_%2820413082204%29.jpg/250px-Subaru_BRZ_engine_%2820413082204%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Subaru_BRZ_engine_(20413082204).jpg)

2012–present [Subaru FA20](/wiki/Subaru_FA_engine "Subaru FA engine")

The first flat engine was produced in 1897 by German engineer [Karl Benz](/wiki/Karl_Benz "Karl Benz").[[5]](#cite_note-English-5)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Called the *kontra* engine, it was a boxer-twin design. Early uses of flat engines in cars include the 1900 [Lanchester 8 hp Phaeton](/wiki/Lanchester_Motor_Company "Lanchester Motor Company") boxer-twin, the 1901 [Wilson-Pilcher](/wiki/Wilson-Pilcher "Wilson-Pilcher") boxer-four,[[12]](#cite_note-Automotor-12) the 1904 Wilson-Pilcher *18/24 HP* boxer-six and the 1903 [Ford Model A](/wiki/Ford_Model_A_(1903%E2%80%931904) "Ford Model A (1903–1904)"), the 1904 [Ford Model C](/wiki/Ford_Model_C "Ford Model C") and the 1905 [Ford Model F](/wiki/Ford_Model_F "Ford Model F").[[13]](#cite_note-Kimes-13)

In 1938, the [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle") (then called the "KdF-Wagen") was released with a rear-mounted flat-four engine. This [Volkswagen air-cooled engine](/wiki/Volkswagen_air-cooled_engine "Volkswagen air-cooled engine") was produced for many years and also used in the [Volkswagen Type 2](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_2 "Volkswagen Type 2") (Transporter, Kombi or Microbus), the [Volkswagen Karmann Ghia](/wiki/Volkswagen_Karmann_Ghia "Volkswagen Karmann Ghia") sports car and the [Volkswagen Type 3](/wiki/Volkswagen_Type_3 "Volkswagen Type 3") compact car. A water-cooled version, known as the [Wasserboxer](/wiki/Volkswagen_Wasserboxer_engine "Volkswagen Wasserboxer engine"), was introduced in 1982 and eventually replaced the air-cooled versions.

Most Porsche sports cars have been powered by flat engines, starting with its first car: the 1948–1965 [Porsche 356](/wiki/Porsche_356 "Porsche 356") used an air-cooled boxer-four engine. Also using boxer-fours were the 1969–1976 [Porsche 914](/wiki/Porsche_914 "Porsche 914"), the 1965–1969 [Porsche 912](/wiki/Porsche_912 "Porsche 912"), and the 2016–present [Porsche Boxster/Cayman (982)](/wiki/Porsche_982 "Porsche 982"). The [Porsche 911](/wiki/Porsche_911_(classic) "Porsche 911 (classic)") has exclusively used boxer-six engines since its introduction, from 1964–present. In 1997, the Porsche 911 changed from being air-cooled to water-cooled.

[Porsche flat-eight engines](/wiki/Porsche_flat-eight_engines "Porsche flat-eight engines") were used in various racing cars throughout the 1960s, such as the 1962 [Porsche 804](/wiki/Porsche_804 "Porsche 804") Formula One car and the 1968–1971 [Porsche 908](/wiki/Porsche_908 "Porsche 908") sports car. Porsche made a flat-twelve engine for the 1969–1973 [Porsche 917](/wiki/Porsche_917 "Porsche 917") sports car.

[Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet") used a horizontally opposed air-cooled 6 cylinder engine in its [Corvair](/wiki/Chevrolet_Corvair "Chevrolet Corvair") line during its entire production run from 1960–1969 in various applications and power ratings, including one of the first uses of a turbocharger in a mass-produced automobile.

The [Subaru EA engine](/wiki/Subaru_EA_engine "Subaru EA engine") was introduced in 1966 and began [Subaru](/wiki/Subaru "Subaru")'s line of boxer-four engines that remain in production to this day.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Most of Subaru's models are powered by a boxer-four engine in either naturally aspirated or turbocharged form. A print ad for the 1973 [Subaru GL](/wiki/Subaru_GL "Subaru GL") coupe referred to the engine as "quadrozontal".[[15]](#cite_note-15) The company also produced boxer-six engines from 1988–1996 and 2001–2019.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2008, the [Subaru EE](/wiki/List_of_Subaru_engines#Subaru_EE_engine_(diesel) "List of Subaru engines") engine became the world's first passenger car diesel boxer engine. This engine is a turbocharged boxer-four with common rail fuel injection.[[4]](#cite_note-Hanlon-4)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Ferrari used [flat-12 engines](/wiki/Ferrari_flat-12_engine "Ferrari flat-12 engine") for various Formula One cars in the 1970s. A road car flat-twelve engine (using a 180-degree V12 configuration) was used for the 1973–1984 [Ferrari Berlinetta Boxer](/wiki/Ferrari_Berlinetta_Boxer "Ferrari Berlinetta Boxer"), 1984–1996 [Ferrari Testarossa](/wiki/Ferrari_Testarossa "Ferrari Testarossa") and their derivatives.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Toyota uses the designation *Toyota 4U-GSE* for the boxer-four engine in the Toyota-badged versions of the *[Toyota 86](/wiki/Toyota_86 "Toyota 86") – Subaru BRZ* twins, although the engine is designed and built by Subaru as the [Subaru FA20](/wiki/Subaru_FA_engine#FA20 "Subaru FA engine").[[3]](#cite_note-Bonk-3)
